name: Bloody Butcher
image_alt: Deep red and burgundy dent corn ears
description: A dramatic heirloom dent corn with deep red to burgundy kernels. Used for cornmeal, grits, and polenta, it produces a slightly sweeter, earthier meal than yellow dent types. Stalks grow 10–12 feet tall.
history: An American heirloom dating to at least 1845, believed to have been developed by Virginia farmers. The intense red pigmentation was unusual in an era when most dent corn was yellow or white.
seasonal_quirks: Very long season — needs a full 100+ days of warmth. Not suitable for short-season climates. Stalks grow exceptionally tall and may need staking in windy locations. Grow for dried corn, not fresh eating.
harvest_cues: For dry corn, leave on stalk until husks are fully papery and kernels are hard. Finish drying indoors in a warm, airy space.
variety_notes: The red pigment is anthocyanin — nutritionally valuable. Ground into red cornmeal for visually striking polenta and grits. Also excellent for corn flour in baking.
preservation: Dry fully and store whole ears. Shell and grind as needed for freshest flavor.
